top_categories.workout_context: Race-pace efforts that mimic Gran Fondo Il Lombardia's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before October.
5,577ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
68 miles on paved — Italian provincial and municipal roads burns energy faster than the distance suggests. Train the fade resistance now.
Gran Fondo Il Lombardia's paved — Italian provincial and municipal roads forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 68 miles.
82ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on paved — Italian provincial and municipal roads. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Gran Fondo Il Lombardia. Train it until it feels boring.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 5,577ft of climbing on paved — Italian provincial and municipal roads does to your power file on race day.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At Gran Fondo Il Lombardia, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.
At Gran Fondo Il Lombardia, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Double-threshold training builds sustained power with less fatigue. More FTP per hour of training.
5-second power closes gaps and wins field sprints. At Gran Fondo Il Lombardia, neuromuscular snap is a tactical tool, not just a finish-line move.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 40–60g carbs/hour for Gran Fondo Il Lombardia’s 68 miles. Front-load calories in the first half. One bottle per hour minimum, more in heat.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Race Simulation, VO2max, and Durability to prepare you for 68 miles of paved — italian provincial and municipal roads in Cantu, Como, Lombardy, Italy.
